questions.text: I tend not to notice when people form temporary alliances within a conversation.
I feel that my personal ambitions should take a back seat to the well-being of those I care about.
I feel that being in control of a situation allows me to do my best work.
I find that when I process others' pain through an objective lens, I am less likely to make decisions that favor the dramatic over the important.
I notice that even reading a particularly moving paragraph in a book can make my throat tighten.
I believe that assembling a life that reflects my personal values and desires is the primary task of adulthood.
I experience the weight of responsibility as something I carry without strain.
I feel that optimizing my workflow for my own efficiency is a top priority.
I ask the group to define what done looks like before we begin.
I find that my emotional state can shift dramatically within the span of minutes.
I can tell when two people in a room are aware of each other even when they are not interacting.
I feel that the weight I give to suffering in my ethical thinking is the quality that has most clearly shaped my sense of what a life well lived actually looks like.
I find that I do not notice when someone deliberately withholds a reaction to maintain power in a conversation.
I tend to work extra hours not for my own advancement, but to lighten the load for my team.
I experience gratitude as a quiet contentment rather than an overwhelming surge.
I plan social events well ahead of time with confirmed dates and details.
I believe that treating what people say as what they mean is a form of respect.
I find that focusing on my own path keeps me grounded and purposeful.
I believe that the obligation to respond to suffering is a moral imperative that applies to me regardless of whether I have a personal connection to those affected.
I can tell when someone is keeping score in a friendship based on how they reference past favors.
When entering a new group, I quickly establish my role as a leader.
I hold the view that the moral concern I feel in response to suffering is the quality that makes my participation in community life meaningful.
I automate or systematize recurring tasks to reduce the chance of human error.
I establish naming conventions for my files so I can search for them easily.
I end my day when I'm done and pick up the thread fresh tomorrow without summarizing.
I feel that my legacy should be defined by the people I helped, not the things I acquired.
I feel a surge of energy when I declare a new direction for a team.
I believe that my sense of inner calm is one of the most stable things about me.
I experience compassion as something that wells up so powerfully it can feel like my chest is cracking open.
I prefer shared responsibility to personal accountability for outcomes.
I am attuned to the way people signal closeness or distance through physical proximity.
I consider it my strength that I can hold space for difficult conversations.
I throw things together at the last minute and it usually works out fine.
I rarely organize finished work because I'd rather move on to the next thing.
I find that engaging with emotional situations analytically, rather than emotionally, consistently leads to more useful and more equitable outcomes.
I think that approaching painful situations with calm objectivity is a form of respect for the complexity of what people are going through.
